source.title: Silver Eagle Refinery Explosion Investigation: Metallurgical Analysis

actions_taken: Site visit and inspection conducted January 7-8, 2010
Second site visit conducted March 29-30, 2010 for pipe sectioning and removal
Ultrasonic thickness testing conducted on fractured pipe segments
Metallographic and quantitative chemical analysis conducted at Exponent laboratory in Phoenix, Arizona
Explosion investigation and analysis conducted by Gregory J. Haussmann, Ph.D., P.E.
Balling Engineering retained to perform site survey activities
event.summary: On November 4, 2009, an explosion occurred in the Mobil Distillate Dewaxing Unit (MDDW) of the Silver Eagle Refinery in Woods Cross, Utah. The unit was undergoing hot hydrogen catalyst regeneration when the 10-inch diameter discharge pipe at the exit of South Reactor R30101 suddenly and completely ruptured. High-pressure gas (81% hydrogen by volume) at 642 psig and 785°F was released and ignited approximately 0.66 seconds after rupture, causing an explosion. The shock wave expanded through the adjacent residential neighborhood, causing blast damage to homes. Metallurgical analysis determined that sulfidation corrosion was the primary cause of significant wall thinning in the 10-inch straight pipe segment, with no documented corrosion monitoring of the straight segment between 1993 and the time of the rupture.

hazards.name: High-pressure hydrogen-rich gas in reactor discharge piping at elevated temperature
Sulfidation corrosion of low-alloy carbon steel piping in high-temperature sulfur-bearing process stream

threats.name: Sulfidation corrosion-induced wall thinning of 10-inch discharge pipe straight segment
Absence of corrosion monitoring of straight pipe segment from 1993 to 2009
Turbulent flow at elbow accelerating localised wall thinning downstream of elbow
Use of susceptible low-alloy steel (low Si, Cr, Mo) in high-temperature sulfur service

consequences.name: Sudden complete rupture of 10-inch reactor discharge pipe
Large-scale release and ignition of high-pressure hydrogen-rich gas cloud
Explosion with blast wave causing structural damage to adjacent residential community
Structural damage to refinery equipment and hydro pad

controls.name: Ultrasonic Thickness (UT) Inspection of Piping
controls.barrier_role: Detect wall thinning due to corrosion before pipe reaches critical thickness
controls.lod_basis: Inspection programme intended to identify corrosion degradation and trigger maintenance action

supporting_text: It appears that corrosion monitoring of the straight segment was not conducted between 1993 and the time of the rupture.
To date, no documentation has been produced indicating that the wall thickness of the straight segment was ever measured from build date to the time of the incident.
In 1993, UT readings on the 10-inch elbow ranged from 0.671 to 0.805 inches.
In October 2007, the UT thickness measurement for this elbow at Wall Thickness Measurement Location 43 was 0.483 inches, indicating wall thickness loss had occurred in the prior 14 years of service.

controls.name: Pressure Vessel and Piping Inspection Programme (Periodic Inspection Sheets)
controls.barrier_role: Periodic formal inspection to verify integrity of pressure-containing components
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory and industry-standard periodic inspection of pressure vessels and associated piping

supporting_text: The October 2007 Inspection Sheet stated an 'original thickness' noted by the inspector as '0.719' AS PER DWG'.
To date, no documentation or design drawings have been produced to show or verify where the inspector determined this original thickness value.
To date, no documentation has been produced indicating that the wall thickness of the straight segment was ever measured from build date to the time of the incident.

controls.name: Material Selection for Corrosion Resistance
controls.barrier_role: Use of corrosion-resistant alloy steel to resist sulfidation and naphthenic acid attack
controls.lod_basis: Inherent material resistance to process stream corrosion mechanisms

supporting_text: Low-alloy carbon steel with low levels of silicon, chromium, and molybdenum are particularly vulnerable.
The low silicon, chromium, and molybdenum contents of the straight segment, and the service temperatures and process stream chemistry, are all consistent with sulfidation corrosion.
The remainder of the line downstream, composed of 8-inch-diameter 300-Series stainless steel, is less susceptible to sulfidation corrosion.

controls.name: Process Pressure and Temperature Monitoring at Reactor Discharge
controls.barrier_role: Monitor operating conditions to detect anomalies indicating loss of containment or process deviation
controls.lod_basis: Instrumentation providing real-time pressure and temperature readings at reactor discharge

supporting_text: Temperature and pressure measurements were made at the discharge of the South Reactor.
At 9:11 a.m., approximately six hours into the procedure, the inlet temperature suddenly dropped, and the exit temperature increased to over 1000°F.
This resulted from the pipe rupture between 9:11 a.m. and 9:12 a.m., causing the inlet temperature to drop as the system depressurized.

controls.name: Security Video Surveillance of MDDW
controls.barrier_role: Provide visual detection of gas release and explosion events for post-incident analysis and emergency response
controls.lod_basis: CCTV surveillance enabling detection of release and timing of ignition

supporting_text: Security video of the accident covered the MDDW.
Analysis of the security video revealed that the time between pipe rupture and the initial explosion was approximately 0.66 seconds.
A frame from this video reveals the initial release of gas from the ruptured pipe.

controls.name: Catalyst Regeneration Operating Procedure
controls.barrier_role: Controlled procedure for hot hydrogen catalyst regeneration to manage process conditions within safe limits
controls.lod_basis: Defined operating procedure governing temperature, pressure, and gas composition during regeneration

supporting_text: Regeneration of the catalyst is a periodically performed procedure, in which the normal liquid hydrocarbon feed is stopped and a hydrogen-rich gas mixture is fed through the catalyst bed.
The process is also heated from a normal operating temperature of 700°F to approximately 800°F.
The reactor inlet temperature rose from 680°F at 9:00 p.m. to 806°F at 3:00 a.m., and remained essentially constant for the start of a planned 24-hour catalyst regeneration cycle.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
